title: Revenue Tracking: New, Expansion, and Churned MRR

questions.question: Why is total MRR described as 'a lie (sort of)'?

options.text: Because flat MRR can hide massive underlying changes — new wins masking churn
Because MRR doesn't include one-time revenue
Because churn is impossible to measure

questions.explanation: Total MRR tells you where you are but hides why. Flat MRR could mean zero change or $3K in new customers, $1K in expansions, and $4K in churn — completely different situations requiring different actions.

questions.question: What should Expansion MRR be as a percentage of net new MRR for a healthy business?
questions.explanation: Expansion MRR should be 20-40% of net new MRR for a healthy business. If you're growing entirely through new customers with no expansion, you have an untapped revenue stream. Existing customers are the cheapest revenue source.

questions.question: Companies with NRR above 100% grow how much faster than those below 100%?
questions.explanation: Companies with Net Revenue Retention above 100% grow 2-3x faster than those below. Existing customers contributing to growth (not just replacement) creates a compounding effect that accelerates everything.

questions.question: If your total MRR grew from $40K to $45K, you can safely assume your business is healthy without checking NRR.
questions.explanation: False. Total MRR growth can mask high churn. You might have $10K in new MRR but $5K in churn — that's an NRR problem hidden behind acquisition. Always decompose MRR and calculate NRR.

questions.question: Break down your revenue into the four MRR components (New, Expansion, Contraction, Churned). Calculate your NRR. What does this decomposition reveal that total MRR alone doesn't show?
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response attempts to categorize their revenue into the four components, calculates or estimates NRR, and identifies a specific insight that the decomposition reveals (e.g., churn masking, no expansion, concentration risk) that total MRR alone would hide.
